proposal.reasoning: The diagnostic report shows that the liquid is too cohesive (density, ΔHvap, surface tension are all high) and the dipole moment is weak because dummy‑bead charges are small and the dummy‑core bonds are very short. To address both issues we (1) make the Lennard‑Jones ε less negative (weaker attraction), (2) increase the core bead σ (rmin) to give a slightly larger excluded volume, (3) lengthen the dummy‑core bond distances while keeping them below σ/2, (4) raise the dummy charges to increase the molecular dipole, and (5) lower the dummy‑core bond force constants just enough to allow the longer equilibrium distance but stay >100 kcal·mol⁻¹·Å⁻² to avoid instability. Four forks are generated to span a range of these adjustments – from a modest weakening of cohesion to a stronger one, and from moderate to larger dipole enhancements – providing both exploration of new regions and exploitation of the most promising direction.

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Making ε less negative reduces intermolecular cohesion, which should lower density, heat of vaporization and surface tension toward the experimental values. Increasing the core bead size (rmin) further decreases packing efficiency, also helping to reduce those properties. Lengthening the dummy‑core bond lengths (r₀) directly increases the separation of opposite charges, thereby raising the dipole moment. Raising the magnitude of the dummy charges (q) amplifies polarity, further increasing the dipole. Slightly lowering the dummy‑core bond force constants keeps the longer bonds stable while still satisfying the >100 kcal·mol⁻¹·Å⁻² requirement.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: Thermodynamic errors (density, ΔHvap, surface tension) will be reduced by 10‑30 % and the dipole moment will move from ~1 D toward the target ~3.7 D, improving the overall objective function.
hypothesis.test_method: The four parameter sets (forks) will be run in parallel simulations; their resulting properties will be compared to the targets to confirm whether the hypothesized trends hold.

The dipole moment is q·d; by keeping the charges at the minimum allowed (±0.55) and shortening the dummy‑core bonds (d) we lower the dipole to the target range without violating the sigma/2 limit. Reducing the dummy bond force constants (k_b) allows the charges to orient more naturally, mitigating the electrostatic pressure. Raising the angle stiffness in one fork tests whether a more rigid dipole orientation influences surface tension.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: The combined changes are expected to (1) produce a stable liquid with realistic density, (2) bring heat of vaporization and surface tension down to experimental values by balancing LJ attraction, and (3) achieve the target dipole moment (~3.7 D) through controlled charge separation. Forks that are more aggressive in epsilon or bond shortening will explore the limits of stability, while the mixed fork will assess the impact of angle rigidity on surface tension.
hypothesis.test_method: Each of the four parameter sets will be run in parallel MD simulations. The resulting density, heat of vaporization, surface tension, and dipole moment at 298 K and 313 K will be compared to the experimental targets. Success is measured by a reduction in the error score and the absence of crash sentinel values.

proposal.reasoning: The last simulations collapsed because Lennard‑Jones attraction was too strong and the excluded‑volume size too small, leading to over‑packing. At the same time the dipole moment was far above target, indicating that the geometry of the dummy charges is too linear and the charge magnitude is too high for the current bond/angle configuration. To resolve these issues we (i) weaken the LJ epsilon (move toward zero) and increase the LJ core size (rmin) to lower cohesion and prevent cell collapse, (ii) shorten the dummy‑core bond angles (theta0) and, where needed, reduce bond lengths to bring the dipole down, (iii) raise the dummy charges to the allowed range (0.5‑0.7) but compensate with smaller angles and shorter bonds, and (iv) increase dummy‑bead force constants in two forks to improve numerical stability. Four forks are generated to span a spectrum: a moderate adjustment, an aggressive weakening of LJ attraction, a stability‑focused fork with high force constants, and a mixed fork that pushes both size and dipole geometry to their limits. This provides exploration of new regions while still exploiting promising directions and avoids the known crash configurations.

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Weakening |epsilon| reduces intermolecular cohesion, preventing the periodic cell from collapsing and lowering the overly high density, heat of vaporization and surface tension. Increasing rmin enlarges the excluded volume, further decreasing packing density. The dipole moment scales with charge magnitude and the separation vector defined by the dummy‑core bond lengths and the angle between opposite dummy charges; decreasing the angle (making the charge vector less linear) and shortening the bonds shortens the effective dipole length, offsetting the increase in charge magnitude required by the parameter bounds. Raising the harmonic spring constants (>100 kcal/mol/Å²) stabilises the dummy‑bead positions and avoids numerical instabilities.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: The combined changes should restore a stable liquid phase (eliminate sentinel 10000 values), bring the bulk density close to the experimental ~0.93 g/cm³, lower heat of vaporization and surface tension to their target ranges, and adjust the dipole moment to ~3.7 D. The stability‑focused fork should also reduce the risk of crashes.
